rationale: In the General Discussion, the authors cite Jerath, Ma, and Park (2014) for a specific prior finding: less popular keywords can signal users closer to purchase and thus higher search-ad value. They then explicitly position their work as building on that stream by adding construal-level matching between query language and ad language. So the citation is used to frame and motivate the paper's contribution, not merely as a passing background mention.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Jerath et al. (2014) as prior evidence on keyword popularity in search advertising and extends that line by proposing and testing construal matching as an additional driver of click outcomes. It argues that abstract/concrete language matching can make even benefit-focused keywords valuable when aligned with consumer mindset.
